meta.title: 3.6 Checkpoint
meta.description: Read stateful components and identify what changes, when, and why.

blocks.content:
## Checkpoint: The Memory

This checkpoint tests whether you have internalized the concepts from Level 3. The goal is to recall and explain these ideas **without scrolling back** to the modules. If you find yourself struggling with a concept, that is valuable information -- revisit that module before moving to Level 4.

### Concept recall

blocks.prompt: Explain the useState pattern. What two things does it return? What happens when you call the setter function? What is the significance of the initial value?
What is a stale closure, and when does it happen? Explain why calling `setCount(count + 1)` three times only increments by one. What is the fix?
Explain the three dependency array patterns for useEffect. For each pattern, describe when the effect runs and give a real-world example.
What problem does useContext solve? Describe the three pieces (create, provide, consume) and name three common things shared through context.

options: A) It reads the user from context, fetches their repos sorted by the selected option, updates the page title, and re-fetches when the user or sort order changes. On first load: render with loading=true, show spinner, both effects run, data arrives, repos display.
B) It fetches repos once on mount and never re-fetches regardless of sort changes.
C) It only runs the effects when the user clicks the sort dropdown.
D) The component crashes because AuthContext is not defined.
problems.explanation: On first load: (1) Component renders -- user comes from context, loading is true, so the Spinner shows. (2) Both useEffects run after render. Effect 1 fetches repos with the default sort ('updated'). Effect 2 sets the page title. (3) When the fetch completes, setRepos and setLoading update state, triggering a re-render that shows the repo list. When the user changes the sort dropdown, setSortBy triggers a re-render, and the first effect re-runs because sortBy is in its dependency array.

options: A) The useEffect has no dependency array, so it runs after every render. The fetch calls `setMessages`, which triggers a re-render, which runs the effect again. Fix: add `[roomId]` as the dependency array.
B) The handleSend function causes an infinite loop by calling setMessages.
C) The input onChange triggers too many re-renders.
D) Using the array index as key causes infinite re-renders.
problems.explanation: The useEffect is missing its dependency array. It runs after every render, fetches messages, calls setMessages, which triggers another render, which runs the effect again -- an infinite loop. The fix is `}, [roomId]);` so the fetch only happens when the room changes. This is the single most common React bug.

options: A) All three `setCount(count + 1)` calls read the same stale `count`. Fix: use functional updates `setCount(prev => prev + 1)` for all three.
B) You cannot call setCount more than once in a function.
C) The count variable is not reactive enough.
D) The button onClick should call setCount directly.
problems.explanation: When incrementThree runs, `count` has whatever value it had when the component last rendered (say, 0). All three calls are `setCount(0 + 1)`. They all set count to 1. The functional update form `setCount(prev => prev + 1)` reads the latest queued value each time: prev is 0, then 1, then 2, resulting in count becoming 3.

options: A) `removeItem` mutates the array with `splice` and passes the same reference to `setItems`. React does not detect the change. Fix: `setItems(items.filter(i => i.id !== id))` or `setItems(prev => prev.filter(i => i.id !== id))`.
B) The findIndex call returns the wrong index.
C) The addItem function is stealing the items reference.
D) The reduce function for calculating total is interfering.
problems.explanation: This is the classic mutation bug from Module 3.2. `items.splice(index, 1)` modifies the existing array in place. `setItems(items)` passes the same array reference. React compares references and may conclude nothing changed, so it does not re-render. The `addItem` function works because it creates a new array with the spread operator. The fix for remove: `setItems(prev => prev.filter(i => i.id !== id))`. You would tell the AI: "The removeItem function mutates the array with splice. Replace it with filter to create a new array."

options: A) It returns `{ messages, isConnected }`. When roomId changes: the cleanup function closes the old WebSocket and resets state, then the effect re-runs to open a new connection to the new room.
B) It returns the WebSocket connection directly.
C) When roomId changes, both connections stay open simultaneously.
D) The hook crashes because you cannot use WebSocket in useEffect.
problems.explanation: The custom hook manages a WebSocket connection. It returns an object with `messages` (an array that grows as messages arrive) and `isConnected` (a boolean). The `[roomId]` dependency means the effect re-runs when the room changes. Before re-running, the cleanup function (`return () => {...}`) closes the old WebSocket, resets the connected flag, and clears old messages. Then a new connection opens for the new room. This is proper cleanup -- exactly what Module 3.3 taught.

blocks.content:
### Self-assessment

Rate your confidence on each Level 3 concept from 1 (cannot explain it) to 5 (could teach it):

| Concept | Confidence (1-5) |
| :-- | :-- |
| useState pattern and the `[value, setValue]` convention | |
| Stale closures and functional updates | |
| Immutable state updates (spread to copy) | |
| useEffect dependency arrays (three patterns) | |
| The infinite loop bug (missing dependency array) | |
| useEffect cleanup functions | |
| useContext and the three pieces of context | |
| Reading custom hooks by name and return value | |
| The State / Effects / JSX reading strategy | |

If any concept is below a 3, revisit that module before continuing.

Level 3 fluency means you can look at a React component with hooks and quickly understand: what it remembers (useState), when it acts (useEffect), what shared data it reads (useContext), and what the user sees (JSX). The most common bugs -- stale closures, mutation, missing dependency arrays -- all follow patterns you can now recognize. You are ready for Level 4.
